event: Upper Paleolithic peoples inhabit the Danube valley, later leaving the carved Venus of Willendorf figurine.
The Hallstatt culture flourishes around its salt-mining center, giving its name to the early European Iron Age.
Celtic tribes settle the eastern Alps and establish the kingdom of Noricum, known for its high-quality iron.
Rome annexes Noricum and the surrounding Alpine and Danubian lands, incorporating them into the empire.
Roman frontier towns such as Vindobona (Vienna) and Carnuntum anchor the Danube limes against Germanic incursions.
Rome cedes parts of Pannonia to the Huns as the western frontier collapses amid migrations.
Slavic and Avar peoples settle the eastern Alpine region, forming the principality of Carantania.
Charlemagne absorbs the region into the Frankish Empire after defeating the Avars and the Bavarian duke.
Emperor Otto II grants the Bavarian Eastern March to the Babenberg dynasty under Margrave Leopold I.
The name 'Ostarrichi', the linguistic root of Osterreich (Austria), appears in an imperial document.
The Privilegium Minus elevates the March of Austria to a hereditary duchy under the Babenbergs.
Austria acquires the Duchy of Styria, expanding Babenberg power in the southeastern Alps.
Rudolf I of Habsburg defeats Ottokar II of Bohemia at the Battle of Marchfeld, beginning Habsburg rule of Austria.
Duke Rudolf IV founds the University of Vienna, one of the oldest universities in the German-speaking world.
The Habsburgs begin their long, near-continuous hold on the title of Holy Roman Emperor.
Austria is formally recognized as an archduchy, elevating the standing of the Habsburg dynasty.
Maximilian I becomes Holy Roman Emperor, expanding Habsburg power through strategic marriages across Europe.
Ottoman forces under Suleiman the Magnificent lay the First Siege of Vienna, which the city withstands.
The Bohemian revolt against Habsburg rule ignites the Thirty Years' War across the Holy Roman Empire.
The Second Siege of Vienna is broken by a relief army, halting Ottoman expansion into Central Europe.
Maria Theresa inherits the Habsburg lands and launches sweeping reforms amid the War of the Austrian Succession.
Mozart's career in Vienna epitomizes the city's emergence as a European capital of classical music.
Francis II proclaims the Austrian Empire in response to Napoleon's imperial ambitions.
The Holy Roman Empire is dissolved, leaving the Austrian Empire as the Habsburgs' core realm.
The Congress of Vienna reorganizes Europe under Austrian Chancellor Metternich's conservative order.
Liberal revolutions sweep the empire, forcing Metternich's resignation and the accession of Franz Joseph I.
Defeat by Prussia at Koniggratz ends Austrian leadership of the German states.
The Ausgleich creates the dual monarchy of Austria-Hungary, sharing sovereignty between Vienna and Budapest.
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo triggers Austria-Hungary's entry into World War I.
Austria-Hungary collapses and the Republic of German-Austria is proclaimed at war's end.
The Treaty of Saint-Germain fixes the small republic's borders and forbids union with Germany.
A brief civil war and an authoritarian coup install the clerical-fascist Federal State of Austria.
Nazi Germany annexes Austria in the Anschluss, ending its independent statehood.
Liberated at the end of World War II, Austria is restored as a republic under four-power Allied occupation.
The Austrian State Treaty restores full sovereignty, and Austria declares permanent neutrality.
Austria joins the United Nations as a neutral state in the heart of Cold War Europe.
Austria signs a free trade agreement with the European Economic Community, deepening Western economic ties.
Vienna becomes a major UN headquarters city with the opening of the Vienna International Centre.
The Waldheim affair over the president's wartime past prompts national reckoning with the Nazi era.
Austria accedes to the European Union following a national referendum.
Austria adopts the euro for accounting as it joins the eurozone with the single currency's launch.
A coalition including Jorg Haider's far-right Freedom Party prompts temporary EU diplomatic sanctions.
Austria becomes a frontline EU state as neighboring Central European countries join the union.
Austria becomes a key transit and reception country during Europe's refugee crisis.
Sebastian Kurz becomes one of the world's youngest government leaders as chancellor.
The COVID-19 pandemic prompts strict lockdowns across Austria.
